chunk_summary: Guidance on minimum meal frequency and portion sizes for complementary feeding in children aged 6–24 months, including specific recommendations for breastfed and non-breastfed infants, practical feeding strategies, and nutrient-dense food preparation tips.
learning_objectives: Understand the minimum meal frequency recommendations for breastfed and non-breastfed children aged 6–24 months.
Learn practical portion size targets for complementary feeding at different age stages.
Identify strategies to improve nutrient density and meal quality for children with limited access to formula or animal milk.
Apply the '24-hour bowl recall' method to assess and improve feeding practices.
key_facts: Breastfed infants need 2–3 meals/day at 6–8 months and 3–4 meals/day at 9–24 months, with 1–2 nutritious snacks as needed.
Non-breastfed children aged 6–24 months require at least 4 feeding occasions/day, including at least one solid or semi-solid meal.
Portion sizes: Start with a few tablespoonfuls at 6 months, progress to half a 250 mL bowl by 9–11 months, and three-quarters to one full bowl by 12–24 months.
Dilution of animal milk reduces nutrient density; focus on thicker, more frequent complementary foods and daily iron-rich foods.
Meals should be spaced 2–3 hours apart during the day, avoiding grazing on sweet liquids between meals.
reference_quote: For breastfed infants, global guidance recommends 2–3 meals/day at 6–8 months and 3–4 meals/day at 9–24 months, with 1–2 nutritious snacks as needed.
document_key_topics: Minimum meal frequency for breastfed and non-breastfed children
Portion size recommendations for complementary feeding
Strategies to improve nutrient density in complementary foods
Assessment of feeding practices using the '24-hour bowl recall' method
